text: The video shows a large, approximately fifteen centimeter intramural myoma within the uterine wall.
The surgeon injects approximately 200 ml of vasopressin into the appropriate plane surrounding the intramural myoma, preparing the tissue for further dissection.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to cut through the seromuscular layer of the uterus, following the injection of a vasopressin solution diluted to five international units in twenty ml of saline.
The surgeon uses an ipsilateral port position to make a transverse incision on the uterine tissue, preparing for the removal of the intramural myoma.
The surgeon observes the effects of vasopressin mixed with diluted saline, noting its role in causing transient ischemia and facilitating hydro dissection around the intramural myoma.
The surgeon uses hydro dissection to create a proper plane around the intramural myoma, gradually separating the tissue layers.
The surgeon uses a myoma screw to fix the myoma, facilitating its enucleation from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon splits away the remaining muscle layer, continuing the dissection process.
The surgeon observes that the ovary appears normal, while the myoma has significantly occupied the entire pelvic region.
The surgeon uses a 10mm myoma screw to securely fix the myoma, allowing for gradual separation from the surrounding uterine tissue.

The surgeon uses a myoma screw to provide anterior traction on the myoma, while employing a harmonic device or suction irrigation instrument to enucleate the myoma from the surrounding uterine tissue.
The surgeon uses a supramolecular port to facilitate the separation of the myoma, applying controlled force to maintain a good plane of dissection.

Approximately seventy-five percent of the myoma has been enucleated, with the remaining portion visible above the uterus. The surgeon repositions the myoma screw multiple times to optimize traction for continued separation of the myoma from the uterine tissue.
The surgeon uses a myoma screw to provide traction, carefully separating the muscle fibers to minimize damage and preserve the muscle plane during the myomectomy.

The surgeon carefully approaches the base of a deep intramural myoma, ensuring the uterine cavity remains closed to avoid additional suturing. Visible feeding vessels are meticulously separated from the myoma base.
The surgeon completes the separation of the entire myoma from the uterine tissue, ensuring that any small, hidden sub-centimeter myomas are also identified and removed.
The myoma has been completely removed, and the surgeon prepares to perform suturing using number one Vicryl, applying a two-layer closure technique to the uterine incision site.
The surgeon begins suturing the uterine muscle, applying one internal layer followed by an external layer, using the muscle tissue to secure the sutures.
The surgeon uses Vicryl suture to enter the serous layer, preparing to secure the tissue. The tail of the suture will be utilized again upon returning to the initial entry point.
The surgeon performs intracorporeal suturing using a continuous suture technique during the laparoscopic myomectomy.

The surgeon begins two-layer suturing using an endoskeletal needle, starting from the right side, moving to the left, and returning to the right. The suturing involves continuous intracorporeal technique with Vicryl suture material.
The surgeon begins suturing the internal layer of the uterine wall using a continuous intracorporeal technique with number one Vicryl suture.
The surgeon uses a number one white needle with Vicryl suture to perform intracorporeal suturing during the myomectomy, avoiding barb sutures due to potential fistulization risks.
The surgeon continues with intracorporeal continuous suturing using the tail of the Vicryl suture, taking multiple bites through the tissue layers. The needle is handled carefully during the process.

The surgeon demonstrates three techniques for orienting the needle during suturing: brushing the needle at the junction of two-thirds and one-third with the upper jaw, transferring the needle from left to right while holding it with the left hand and pulling the tail with the right hand, and hanging the needle like a pendulum before crossing it towards the right.
The surgeon begins suturing the internal layer, moving the needle from right to left in a planned two-layer suturing technique using an endoskeletal needle.
The surgeon continues the suturing process, positioning the needle perpendicular to the tissue for ipsilateral suturing. The technique is suitable for a transverse incision.
The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector to hold the tip of the needle during the suturing process in the laparoscopic myomectomy.

The surgeon slowly approximates the muscle layer during suturing in the laparoscopic myomectomy. Minimal bleeding is observed, which is expected to decrease or stop once both layers of suturing are completed.

The surgeon takes the needle out from the serosa, passing through the internal layer on the healthy serosa, and then returns back to complete the second layer of suturing using a baseball stitch technique.
The surgeon uses the suture as traction to take bites for approximating the outer layer, bringing the edges of the tissue together.

The surgeon uses the open jaw of the needle holder to tighten the suture, while the left hand instrument lifts the suture. The upper jaw of the needle holder presses to erect the needle, allowing the surgeon to continue taking bites through the serosal layer.
The surgeon completes the suturing of the uterus, ensuring the myoma is removed and the uterine shape is restored, with smooth margins to prevent adhesion formation.

The surgical field shows minimal blood loss throughout the myomectomy procedure, with visible small pools of blood present. No suction irrigation has been used so far, and the remaining blood will be removed at the end of the surgery.
The surgeon continues to perform continuous suturing on the uterine tissue, ensuring that the sutures are placed in healthy tissue areas to maintain structural integrity.
The surgeon takes a deep bite into the healthy serosa using a surgical needle, applying adequate pressure to secure the tissue. The left hand supports the area during this maneuver.
The surgeon pulls the suture tail to tighten the inner layer during laparoscopic myomectomy, demonstrating the use of both intracorporeal and extracorporeal knot techniques to secure the sutures.
The surgeon forms a C-shaped loop with the suture and applies a double wrap to secure it, using a surgeon's knot. A tumble square knot may also be used for tying.

The surgeon performs a final knot tying during the laparoscopic myomectomy, using a technique that involves multiple wraps to secure the suture. The end of the suture is cut after the knot is completed.
The surgeon completes the suturing and carefully cuts the suture. Suction and irrigation are performed to wash out collected blood, ensuring the surgical area is clean and properly visualized.

The surgeon begins the process of morselation, using a morselator to gradually fragment the myoma tissue. The morselator tip is positioned in a neutral area, ensuring that the tissue is brought to the morselator for safe and controlled morselation.
